title: Pie and chips
subtitle: A shortcrust beef and gravy pie with oven chips and mushy peas
excerpt: Pie and chips is the pub dinner plate in its simplest form. A shortcrust pastry pie filled with beef and gravy, served with oven chips and mushy peas or baked beans. The pie can be made ahead; the chips cook in the oven alongside it.
sourceNotes: Classic British pub and home dinner combination. Pie and chips has been a standard British pub meal since at least the mid-20th century, combining the national love of both pastry pies and fried potato.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unbaked, assembled pie for up to 3 months. Bake from frozen at 180°C for 55 minutes, foil-covered for the first 20 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made 2 days ahead and chilled. Assemble and bake on the day.

content.text: Pie and chips is the most satisfying combination on a pub menu, and it is simpler to make at home than most people expect. Ready-rolled shortcrust pastry makes the pie achievable on a weeknight, and the filling only needs 30 minutes to simmer while you prepare the rest. The chips go in the oven at the same temperature as the pie so everything finishes together.

Heat the oil in a large saucepan over a high heat. Brown the beef in batches until dark on all sides. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium, add the onion, and cook for 8 minutes until softened.

Stir in the flour and cook for 1 minute. Return the beef to the pan with the stock and Worcestershire sauce. Season well. Simmer for 30 minutes until the gravy is thick and the beef is tender. Cool slightly.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line a deep baking tin with two-thirds of the pastry, letting it hang over the sides. Spoon in the cooled filling. Lay the remaining pastry over the top, press the edges together, and trim.
Brush the top with beaten egg. Make a small cut in the centre to let steam escape. Chill for 30 minutes if time allows.

Spread the chips on a separate tray. Put both the pie and the chips in the oven. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the chips are crisp. Rest the pie for 5 minutes before cutting.

items.cause: Filling was too hot when added, or too wet.
items.fix: Allow the filling to cool before assembling. Reduce the filling further if the gravy is very loose.

items.symptom: The pastry lid shrinks away from the edges
items.fix: Rest the assembled pie in the fridge for 30 minutes before baking. This relaxes the gluten and prevents shrinkage.

items.symptom: The pie leaks gravy during baking
items.cause: Edges not sealed tightly, or filling was overfilled.
items.fix: Press the pastry edges firmly together and crimp. Leave a small gap at the top of the filling rather than packing to the brim.

items.symptom: The chips and pie are not ready at the same time
items.cause: Chips placed in the oven at a different time.
items.fix: Put both trays in at the same time. Chips at this temperature take 25 to 30 minutes, which matches the pie finishing time.

content.text: Pie and chips is the canonical British pub dinner. Every blackboard menu has a version: pastry, filling, and fried potato on the same plate. The British love of pie is ancient. The pairing with chips dates from the postwar period, when oven-ready chips made it practical at home as well as in the pub.
